learning_outcomes.description: Understand Indigenous Peoples rights, as defined in the specific relevance for UNESCO’s work.
Understand the effects of colonisation/globalisation and its varied manifestations internationally in relation to theory concerning the international struggles of Indigenous groups.
Understand cultural heritage, knowledge, traditional cultural expressions and languages of Indigenous peoples globally.

Engage with and understand theoretical underpinnings and debates, curriculum and policy frameworks, and pedagogical practices which make up the landscape of Indigenous education in Australia today through Australian Indigenous academics and elders.
Understand and continually reflect upon your professional, political and personal positioning and practice as an educator in relation to Indigenous students, education, peoples and cultures.
Demonstrate an understanding and positioning as a teacher in relation to working with Indigenous students.

assessments.due_date:
Written Reflective Learning Journal - Due 2pm 14/09/2026

Viva / Oral Reflection 14/09/2026 - 21/09/2026
assessments.task_description:
OVERVIEW

This assessment requires students to critically reflect on their learning across the unit using the Five Rs Framework (Bain et al., 1999). Students will demonstrate how their engagement with Indigenous education theories, learning materials, and their own social positioning informs their development as teachers committed to respect, trust, and positive relationships.


Part A: Written Reflective Learning Journal - Task Requirements

Students will produce five reflective entries, each 400 words (total 2000 words). Each entry must apply the Five Rs:

·     Reporting - Identify the learning source (lecture, tutorial, reading, online activity).

·     Responding - Describe initial reactions, emotions, and thoughts.

·     Relating - Connect the learning to personal, cultural, social, or professional experiences.

·     Reasoning - Analyse the learning using relevant theories (e.g., Critical Race Theory, Indigenous Standpoint Theory, Cultural Interface).

·     Reconstructing - Explain how this learning will shape future teaching practice.


Evidence of Learning

Reflections must demonstrate:

·     Engagement with multiple learning sources

·     Integration of key theories

·     Use of Indigenous-authored scholarship

·     Indigenous-authored sources must be cited appropriately.

·     Critical examination of social positioning

·     Clear articulation of implications for future teaching practice


Writing Style

·     First-person reflective voice

·     Short, clear paragraphs

·     Academic yet personal tone

·     Referencing where appropriate


Part B: Viva / Oral Reflection

Mandatory oral defence of submitted work.

The viva is a short, structured oral examination in which you will be asked to discuss about your reflective journal entries. It serves to authenticate your work and demonstrate that you have developed genuine command of the theoretical and practical content of your reflection using the 5E approach. The viva is not an assessment of new knowledge — it is an opportunity to show the thinking and understanding behind the work you have already submitted.


FORMAT AND LOGISTICS                 

Duration

10–15 minutes per student

Format

Individual; conducted in person or via video conference for external students (Zoom)

Conducted by

Course lecturer and/or a second academic staff member

Scheduling

Within one week of the submission deadline, you will receive a time-slot allocation

Preparation

Review your submitted report in full; be ready to discuss any section of the reflection in depth.

Permitted materials

A printed copy of your submitted report only; no other notes or devices permitted


ASSESSMENT FOCUS

The viva will be assessed on:

·     Depth of critical reflection

·     Ability to synthesise theory and practice

·     Understanding of Indigenous perspectives and relational accountability
assessments.submission_guidelines: Written reflective journal will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Suggested Topics

Select an issue from the list below and undertake a research essay demonstrating your understanding of the content using an interdisciplinary approach.


Culture and Identity

How Indigenous cultural identity is constructed, maintained, disrupted, and reclaimed in the context of colonisation, assimilation policies, and contemporary education.

Stolen Generations

The forced removal of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children from their families, and the enduring consequences for education, health, law, and identity.

Indigenous Policy

The development, implementation, and impact of Australian government policies affecting Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples, including their educational dimensions.

Race Issues

How race and racism operate within educational and social institutions in Australia, drawing on Critical Race Theory and Indigenous scholarship.

Indigenous Languages

The historical suppression and contemporary revitalisation of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander languages, and their role in education and cultural maintenance.

Indigenous Health

The social, historical, and cultural determinants of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health, including the role of education in health outcomes.

Indigenous Human Rights (Global/Australia)

Indigenous human rights frameworks — including UNDRIP — in relation to education, self-determination, and sovereignty in Australia and globally.

Land Rights

The history of land rights in Australia, including the Mabo decision and native title, and their intersection with identity, culture, and education.

Choose a topic that interest you and you can discuss this with your co-ordinator/tutor

 

 Required Elements -

Your essay must explicitly address all three of the following required elements. Element 4 is optional.

Element 1- How your chosen topic is connected to Indigenous education in Australia.

Element 2- How history and colonisation is implicated in your chosen topic.

Element 3- Global Indigenous and/or Aboriginal & Torres Strait Islander perspectives on the topic.

Element 4- Interdisciplinary perspectives on the topic (e.g., health, education, law, sociology, psychology, policy) -optional


Recommended Essay Structure

You are not required to follow this structure rigidly, but it is recommended to ensure all elements are addressed.

Section

Word guide

What to include

Introduction

Introduce topic, state argument, outline the four elements.

Element 1 — Connection to Indigenous Education

Explain how your topic is directly connected to Indigenous education in Australia. Use scholarly literature.

Element 2 — History and Colonisation

Discuss how colonisation and historical policy-making shaped the issue. Be specific about periods and mechanisms.

Element 3 — Indigenous & Global Perspectives

Centre the perspectives of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. Compare with global Indigenous experiences.

Conclusion

Synthesise your argument across all four elements. Do not introduce new information.

Reference List

Not counted

APA 7th edition format. Minimum 12 scholarly sources.

learning_activities.topic:
Wk 1-Introducing Global Indigenous knowledges & Education

The first lecture of the course is a positioning lecture where we provide an overview and an introduction to the landscape of Global Indigenous education incorporating Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Studies.
This introductory lecture will discuss use of appropriate language in engaging with the course content and the key theories and policies that have informed the development of the course. The assessment tasks for the course will also be outlined.

This introductory lecture will discuss use of appropriate language in engaging with the course content and the key theories and policies that have informed the development of the course. The assessment tasks for the course will also be outlined.

learning_activities.topic:
Wk 1:Introducing Global Indigenous knowledges & Ed

Week 1: Our place, our history, our identities: The first tutorial will start off with understanding who you are within this space. We will introduce you to the 5 Rs through undertaking a critical self-reflection activity based on lecture one. We will also explore how our social, cultural and historical experiences shape our lenses and worldviews.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2- A Global History of Colonisation

Aboriginality, Blackness and the Formation of the Colonial Matrix of Power. Discussion on the dominance of the majority culture.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 2- A Global History of Colonisation Aboriginality, Blackness and the Formation of the Colonial Matrix of Power. Discussion on the dominance of the majority culture.

learning_activities.topic:
Wk 2: A Global History of Colonisation

Examining the impact of colonisation. Watch the video-Reconciling our histories, Myall Creek Massacre and engage in a discussion relating it to some of the global atrocities around the world. For e.g. The Rohingya genocide which is a series of ongoing persecutions by the Myanmar military of the Muslim Rohingya people.

Wk 3-Exploring Race Issues in Indigenous Education

This week we take a theoretical step back to think deeply about the centrality of race to our work as teachers in the landscape of Indigenous education through the lens of critical race theory.

Wk 3- Exploring Race Issues in Indigenous Education

Exploring issues on race, whiteness and privilege- Personal & Pedagogical. the overarching topic is on Race/ism and what it means for our praxis as teachers. We will also Un/entangle the lecture and the readings: What is race-making?

Exploring Indigenous identities in New Zealand & Australia. Looking at how teachers can affirm identities of Indigenous Young People.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5 - Growing up Torres Strait Islander, Navigating the complexities of culture & Identity

Dr Antoinette Cole will share this resource that was developed to provide the foundation for teachers and students in Australian schools to challenge ongoing negative discourse about Indigenous Australians. In Australia, Indigenous people are a minority, making up approximately 3 per cent of the Australian population. To ensure Torres Strait Islander people’s knowledge and perspectives are authentically incorporated into learning experiences for all Australian students, it is critical that their voices and experiences be included in the Australian Curriculum.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7-Histories and the importance of communities. Guest Lecturer: Aunty Denise Proud, Honorary Senior Fellow, UQ

Aunty Denise is a proud Aboriginal woman and respected Elder and educator, known nationally and internationally for her work as an artist, author and presenter. Aunty Denise is also an expert consultant in Indigenous and early childhood education. Aunty Denise will share her personal experience of living through past policies in Queensland on the settlement of Cherbourg and her journey of becoming a successful educator in diverse settings. https://education.uq.edu.au/profile/2448/denise-proud.
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 7-Histories and the importance of communities. Guest Lecturer: Aunty Denise Proud, Honorary Senior Fellow, UQ

Aunty Denise will share her personal experience of living through past policies in Queensland on the settlement of Cherbourg and her journey of becoming a successful educator in diverse settings. https://education.uq.edu.au/profile/2448/denise-proud .

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7- Histories and the importance of understanding

We will unpack the lecture content shared by Aunty Denise on Australian histories - how much do we really know? Students will explore Australian histories and critically reflect on their own context, why we know or don't know and how we have come to know.

learning_activities.topic:
Wk 8-The stolen generation & exemption certificate (Identity Constructs) : Guest Lecturer: Aunty Judi Wickes

Professor Aunty Judi Wickes (Kalkadoon/Wakka Wakka) is a respected community Elder, social worker and educator who write and presents nationally and internationally on her research on the impact of the stolen generation and the certificate of exemption. Aunty Judi will share her research and her story about her many years working as a social worker in community organisations and in education settings.

Aunty Judi will share her research and her story about her many years working as a social worker in community organisations and in education settings.

learning_activities.topic:
Wk 10: Lost Culture & Languages

This lecture focuses on language extinction, and the impact it has on culture. Every language reflects a unique worldview with its own value systems, philosophy and particular cultural features. The extinction of a language results in the irrecoverable loss of unique cultural knowledge embodied in it for centuries, including historical, spiritual and ecological knowledge that may be essential for the survival of not only its speakers, but also countless others. Hunt, 2018. This lecture focuses on the impact of language loss and the importance of language revitalisation. Guest speaker: Naomi Fillmore.

The extinction of a language results in the irrecoverable loss of unique cultural knowledge embodied in it for centuries, including historical, spiritual and ecological knowledge that may be essential for the survival of not only its speakers, but also countless others. Hunt, 2018. This lecture focuses on the impact of language loss and the importance of language revitalisation. Guest speaker: Naomi Fillmore.

learning_activities.topic:
Wk 10- Languages & Indigenous Languages

Brown Paper Bag - Boori Monty Pryor: This tutorial includes a special screening of a film made by renowned Aboriginal author, Boori Monty Pryor. The film Brown Paper Bag explores assumptions made by teachers that can impact on how Indigenous students see themselves and how they engage with curriculum.
